aliases: Chinese hot and sour wheat noodles

english: Chinese hot and sour wheat noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for suan la mian; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315671) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for suan la mian; A00LC (wheat noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible analogue for the noodle base component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for suan la mian or hot and sour noodles as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of suan la mian is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as commonly served in Chinese noodle restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Suan La Mian — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Suan La Mian (CR ID F05060126). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked wheat noodles with chili oil dressing (USDA SR Legacy #20121 and #04042) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for suan la mian. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles (white, boiled), which reports GI ~53 (medium). The vinegar/acid component in the dressing is known to lower postprandial glycaemic response by approximately 20–30% (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA); however, the conservative unadjusted wheat noodle GI of 53 is retained to avoid over-optimism. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 35.0) / 100 = 18.6, using 350 g serving × 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 35 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 349 mg per 100 g; a 350 g bowl delivers approximately 1222 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit suan la mian to a half-portion (≤175 g) and request reduced soy sauce and pickled vegetables; avoid adding extra condiments or soup broth to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with GI ~53 and low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) result in a glycemic load of approximately 18.6 per standard 350 g serving, which is in the medium-high range for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce portion size to 200 g or fewer, request wholemeal or konjac noodle substitutes to lower GI and increase fibre, and note that the vinegar component provides a modest glycaemic-dampening effect that does not fully compensate for refined wheat noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 349 mg/100 g and chili oil contributing saturated fat (~0.45 g/100 g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; total fat is moderate at 2.84 g/100 g but the sodium load per serving is clinically significant.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 200 g per meal, request reduced chili oil and soy sauce, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal to keep total daily sodium below 1500 mg as recommended by the American Heart Association.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chili oil (nightshade-derived capsaicin), high sodium from pickled vegetables, and refined wheat noodles with low fibre collectively contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request a chili-free version, reduce or omit pickled vegetables, and consider substituting wheat noodles with glass noodles or konjac noodles to lower glycaemic load and reduce solanine exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Suan La Mian (酸辣面, literally 'sour-spicy noodles') is a popular Chinese wheat noodle dish originating from Sichuan and Chongqing provinces. The dish consists of boiled wheat noodles served in a flavourful broth or dressed with a sauce combining chili oil, black vinegar (Chinkiang vinegar), soy sauce, sesame paste, garlic, and pickled vegetables such as ya cai (Yibin preserved mustard greens). The flavour profile is characteristically bold — simultaneously sour, spicy, savoury, and slightly nutty from sesame. The texture features soft, slightly chewy wheat noodles in a thin, oily, aromatic broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 78 kcal, 3 g protein, 2.84 g fat, and 10 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre and 349 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on the wheat noodle base, with a glycemic load of approximately 18.6 per standard 350 g bowl. Clinically, the primary concerns are moderate-to-high sodium content (a full bowl delivers ~1222 mg sodium) and a low-to-medium glycemic load from refined wheat noodles with low fibre. The vinegar component provides a modest glycaemic-dampening effect. The dish is suitable for general consumption in controlled portions but requires modification for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is vegetarian and vegan-friendly in its standard preparation, contains gluten and soy allergens, and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course in Chinese restaurants and hawker settings across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

Can diabetics eat suan la mian?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060126) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entries and documented as adapted. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry and documented as analogous estimate. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific suan la mian preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat (0.45 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles (#20121) and chili oil (#04042) in proportions consistent with the dish composition; this is not directly reported by HPB. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat noodles (boiled); no direct GI measurement for suan la mian exists in approved sources. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under primary_cuisine 'western' and ethnic_tags 'western'; corrected to Chinese cuisine with Sichuan/Chongqing origin. 5. Dietary flags: vegetarian and vegan status assumes standard preparation without meat or egg toppings; individual restaurant preparations may vary. 6. Halal status is conditionally true assuming no pork-derived ingredients in the chili oil or pickled vegetables; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
